---
title: "Learn panel data"
jupyter: python3
---

```{=html}
<p style="margin: 0.25rem 0 1.25rem;">
  <strong>Run this page interactively in Google Colab — no install required:</strong><br>
  <a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/cmg777/expdpy/blob/main/notebooks/learn.ipynb" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
    <img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab">
  </a>
</p>
```

The **Learn** module is the teaching layer. Every result speaks plain language through
`.interpret()` and `.explain()`; **concept sandboxes** simulate data so you can *see* and tune
an idea; and **concept explainers** give a what / when / caveats summary for every method.

This page is a short guided tour of the core panel-data ideas — first differences, demeaning
and dummy variables, and the fixed-vs-random-effects choice — followed by the full sandbox
and explainer galleries.

```{python}
import expdpy as ex
from expdpy.data import load_kuznets, load_kuznets_data_def

# `set_labels` attaches the data dictionary's human-readable labels, so the figures and
# tables on this page read with full labels.
df = ex.set_labels(load_kuznets(), load_kuznets_data_def())
```

## Read a model in plain language

Fit a model anywhere in [Analyze](analyze.qmd), then ask it to explain itself. `.interpret()`
gives an **associational** reading (never a causal claim unless the design supports it):

```{python}
res = ex.analyze_regression_table(
    df,
    dvs="gini_regional",
    idvs=["log_gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc_sq", "log_gdp_pc_cu"],
    feffects=["country", "year"],
    clusters=["country"],
)
print(res.interpret())
```

`.explain()` — or `ex.explain(topic)` — returns a concept explainer; `ex.list_topics()` lists
every topic:

```{python}
ex.explain("fixed_effects")
```

## The core identity: first differences ≈ demeaning ≈ dummy variables

A unit fixed effect is anything constant within a unit over time. Three transformations all
**remove** it and recover the same within-unit slope:

- **First differences** subtract each unit's previous-period value (Δy on Δx).
- **The within transformation (demeaning)** subtracts each unit's time-average.
- **Least-squares dummy variables (LSDV)** add one dummy per unit to an OLS regression.

`learn_first_differences` simulates a panel where the regressor is correlated with the unit
effect (so pooled OLS is biased) and recovers the slope by first differences and by demeaning.
On a two-period panel the two coincide exactly, and both recover the truth:

```{python}
fd = ex.learn_first_differences(n_periods=2)
print(fd.interpret())
fd.fig
```

`learn_within_vs_lsdv` shows that demeaning and unit dummies give the **identical** slope —
the Frisch–Waugh–Lovell theorem at work — for any number of periods:

```{python}
wl = ex.learn_within_vs_lsdv(n_periods=6)
print(wl.interpret())
wl.fig
```

The matching explainers spell out when to reach for each:

```{python}
ex.explain("first_differences")
```

```{python}
ex.explain("dummy_variables")
```

## Fixed vs random effects, and the Mundlak bridge

Pooled OLS is biased when the unit effects are correlated with the regressor; fixed effects
fix it by using only within-unit variation:

```{python}
pf = ex.learn_pooled_vs_fixed_effects(unit_effect_corr=0.8)
print(pf.interpret())
pf.fig
```

The **correlated random effects (Mundlak)** estimator builds a bridge between the two — see
its explainer, and `analyze_cre_table` in [Analyze](analyze.qmd):

```{python}
ex.explain("correlated_random_effects")
```

## Two more classics

**Omitted-variable bias** — what happens to a coefficient when a correlated confounder is left
out:

```{python}
ex.learn_omitted_variable_bias(corr_xz=0.6).fig
```

**Clustered standard errors** — why ignoring within-cluster correlation understates
uncertainty:

```{python}
ex.learn_clustering_se(icc=0.3).fig
```

## Explainer index

`ex.list_topics()` returns every registered concept explainer; pass any of them (or an alias)
to `ex.explain(...)`:

```{python}
ex.list_topics()
```

```{python}
print(ex.explain("clustered_se"))
```

## Sandbox gallery

Every sandbox returns a figure, a small comparison table (`.df`), a `summary` of the facts it
turns on, and a plain-language `.interpret()`.

### `learn_omitted_variable_bias`

```{python}
ex.learn_omitted_variable_bias(corr_xz=0.6).fig
```

### `learn_pooled_vs_fixed_effects`

```{python}
ex.learn_pooled_vs_fixed_effects(unit_effect_corr=0.8).fig
```

### `learn_clustering_se`

```{python}
ex.learn_clustering_se(icc=0.4).fig
```

### `learn_first_differences`

```{python}
ex.learn_first_differences(n_periods=2).fig
```

### `learn_within_vs_lsdv`

```{python}
ex.learn_within_vs_lsdv(n_periods=6).fig
```

### `learn_beta_convergence`

Absolute vs. **conditional** convergence on a known-parameter AR(1) panel: omitting a
steady-state determinant biases the unconditional slope; conditioning on it recovers the true
convergence slope. See [`analyze_beta_convergence`](analyze_beta_convergence.qmd) for the full
analysis on real data.

```{python}
bc = ex.learn_beta_convergence(rho=0.9, gamma=0.6, corr=0.7)
print(bc.interpret())
bc.fig
```

### `learn_sigma_convergence`

σ-convergence on a panel whose cross-sectional dispersion narrows at a **known** rate: every
unit's value contracts toward a common mean, so the standard deviation, the Gini index and the
coefficient of variation all shrink at the log-rate `ln(rho)` — and the function recovers it. See
[`analyze_sigma_convergence`](analyze_sigma_convergence.qmd) for the full analysis on real data.

```{python}
sc = ex.learn_sigma_convergence(rho=0.93)
print(sc.interpret())
sc.fig
```

### `learn_convergence_clubs`

Club convergence on a panel with a **planted** structure: each unit belongs to one of several
clubs that converge to distinct levels, so the panel does not converge globally but the
Phillips–Sul clustering algorithm recovers the clubs — without being told they exist. See
[`analyze_convergence_clubs`](analyze_convergence_clubs.qmd) for the full workflow on real data.

```{python}
cc = ex.learn_convergence_clubs()
print(cc.interpret())
cc.fig
```

## Where to go next

- [Explore panel data](explore.qmd) — describe your data first.
- [Analyze panel data](analyze.qmd) — the estimators these ideas underpin.
- Prefer no code? Launch the [Learn app](streamlit.qmd) — the sandboxes and explainers, interactive.
